text:
GENITOURINARY MEDICAL ONCOLOGY — FOLLOW-UP VISIT

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Genitourinary Oncology Attending]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Rising PSA over the past three months with worsening back pain and new right hip pain. Return visit for reassessment of disease status on enzalutamide (Xtandi).

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with a well-established diagnosis of metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), Gleason 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5, with documented germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT), who presents today for follow-up approximately six months after initiating enzalutamide 160 mg orally once daily. Castration-resistant progression was established roughly six months ago when his PSA rose from a nadir of 0.8 ng/mL to 4.7 ng/mL over three consecutive monthly measurements while castrate testosterone was confirmed, after approximately 24 months on leuprolide plus abiraterone/prednisone. At that time, restaging PSMA PET/CT demonstrated progression with new osseous lesions at T10, bilateral ribs, and the right sacral ala, with enlargement of the right obturator lymph node. He was transitioned to enzalutamide 160 mg daily and his PSA initially responded favorably, declining to a nadir of 0.6 ng/mL at approximately five months. Denosumab was added at the time of enzalutamide initiation for bone-targeted therapy.

Unfortunately, over the past approximately six to eight weeks, Mr. Kowalski has noted a clear worsening of his previously controlled lumbar back pain, now rating it as 4-5/10 at baseline with exacerbations up to 7/10 with prolonged ambulation. He reports the pain localizes primarily to the L3-L4 region, similar in character to his prior symptoms but of greater intensity. Of greater concern is a new onset of right hip and groin pain that began approximately one month ago. He describes this as a dull aching discomfort, rated 3-4/10 at rest, worsening with weight-bearing activity. He has resumed scheduled acetaminophen 1000 mg three times daily and has required ibuprofen on several occasions, which he takes cautiously given his history. He has not yet required opioid analgesics.

He denies new weakness, numbness, or tingling in the lower extremities. No bowel or bladder dysfunction. No new headaches or focal neurological symptoms. No dyspnea, chest pain, or hemoptysis. No abdominal pain or nausea. No hematuria. He reports a modest decrease in his appetite over the past month but denies significant weight loss. Energy level has declined compared with his best performance three months ago.

His last PSA laboratory result obtained three weeks ago was 6.8 ng/mL, which had risen from 4.2 ng/mL the prior month and 2.9 ng/mL the month before that — three consecutive rises from the 0.6 ng/mL nadir, fulfilling biochemical progression criteria. Today's PSA is pending but expected to be similar or higher.

He is currently on:
- Leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months (last administered approximately 6 weeks ago, next dose due in approximately 6 weeks)
- Enzalutamide 160 mg orally once daily
- Denosumab 120 mg subcutaneous every 4 weeks (last dose 2 weeks ago)
- Calcium carbonate 1250 mg twice daily
- Vitamin D3 2000 IU daily
- Amlodipine 5 mg daily and lisinopril 10 mg daily for hypertension
- Metformin 1000 mg twice daily for type 2 diabetes
- Atorvastatin 40 mg daily for hyperlipidemia
- Acetaminophen 1000 mg three times daily (scheduled) for bone pain
- Omeprazole 20 mg daily

He previously completed physical therapy for fall prevention; he reports one near-fall in the last month while walking on uneven ground, which he attributes to pain-related gait alteration.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Mild fatigue (Grade 1-2), decreased appetite, no significant weight loss reported (will verify weight today). No fevers, no night sweats, no rigors.
Musculoskeletal: Worsening lumbar back pain (L3-L4 region, 4-5/10 baseline), new right hip and groin pain (3-4/10), no new joint swelling.
Neurological: Denies lower extremity weakness, sensory changes, or gait abnormality beyond pain-limited walking. No cognitive changes noted since Grade 1 cognitive slowing previously attributed to enzalutamide, which has been stable.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations. Blood pressure has been well controlled per home monitoring.
Respiratory: No dyspnea at rest. No cough, no hemoptysis.
Gastrointestinal: Mild decreased appetite, no nausea, no vomiting. Bowel function normal, no rectal bleeding.
Genitourinary: No dysuria, no hematuria, no changes in urinary function. No urinary incontinence.
Endocrine/Metabolic: Ongoing hot flashes related to ADT (Grade 1, manageable). No polyuria, polydipsia (diabetes well-controlled per recent labs).
Psychiatric: Anxious about disease status today. Reports adequate sleep.
Dermatologic: No new rashes.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 138/82 mmHg, heart rate 74 bpm, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, temperature 98.2°F, weight 174 lbs (down approximately 3 lbs from last visit six weeks ago), BMI 24.8.

General: Mr. Kowalski is a well-appearing, alert and oriented male in no acute distress. He is ambulatory with a slightly antalgic gait favoring the right lower extremity. He appears mildly fatigued but is engaged and conversational.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear. No scleral icterus. Conjunctivae pale to mildly pale bilaterally.

Neck: Supple. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy appreciated.

Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, crackles, or rhonchi.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity edema.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild diffuse tenderness on deep palpation of the right lower quadrant. No guarding, no rebound. No hepatomegaly appreciated on palpation. No splenomegaly.

Musculoskeletal: Tenderness to palpation over the L3-L4 spinous processes and paraspinal musculature. Tenderness to palpation over the right greater trochanteric region and along the right groin with passive range of motion of the right hip limited by pain (approximately 90 degrees of flexion, with internal and external rotation limited to roughly 20-25 degrees due to discomfort). No visible deformity or swelling of the right hip or proximal thigh.

Lower Extremities: 5/5 motor strength bilaterally in hip flexors, knee extension, dorsiflexion, and plantar flexion. No lower extremity edema. Sensation intact to light touch.

Lymph Nodes: No palpable inguinal, axillary, or cervical lymphadenopathy detected on examination.

Skin: No rashes, no jaundice.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Today's results (as available):
- PSA: 8.3 ng/mL (markedly elevated from nadir of 0.6 ng/mL; rising over past three monthly measurements — confirmed PSA progression)
- Testosterone: < 20 ng/dL (castrate, confirmed)
- CBC: Hemoglobin 11.1 g/dL (mildly decreased), MCV 84 fL, platelets 212 K/uL, WBC 6.4 K/uL with normal differential
- Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.2 mEq/L, creatinine 1.0 mg/dL (eGFR >60), total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, AST 34 U/L, ALT 29 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 148 U/L (mildly elevated, likely reflecting osseous disease), albumin 3.9 g/dL
- LDH: 218 U/L (mildly elevated)
- HbA1c: 7.1% (diabetes well-controlled)
- Lipid panel: within goal on statin

Prior values for context:
- PSA nadir on enzalutamide: 0.6 ng/mL (~5 months ago)
- PSA at +4 months: 2.9 ng/mL
- PSA at +5 months: 4.2 ng/mL (obtained 3 weeks prior to today)
- PSA at +6 months (today): 8.3 ng/mL

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Most recent prior imaging (bone scan and CT abdomen/pelvis, approximately five months after starting enzalutamide): Osseous lesions at L3, L4, T10, bilateral ribs, and sacrum appeared stable with sclerotic changes. Right obturator lymph node measured 0.9 cm (down from 1.4 cm at CRPC progression). No visceral metastases at that time.

Restaging imaging has been ordered today given clinical and biochemical evidence of progression (see plan below).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. METASTATIC CASTRATION-RESISTANT PROSTATE CANCER — PROGRESSION ON ENZALUTAMIDE

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with mCRPC, Gleason 9, Grade Group 5, with germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (biallelic loss confirmed on prior somatic testing), who has experienced clear biochemical progression on enzalutamide. His PSA has risen from a nadir of 0.6 ng/mL to 8.3 ng/mL over three consecutive monthly measurements — a rise of 7.7 ng/mL and greater than a 25% increase above nadir, meeting standard PCWG3-aligned PSA progression criteria. This is in the setting of confirmed castrate testosterone levels, so cross-resistance within the AR-signaling pathway is likely contributing. The new right hip pain in the context of a known right femoral neck osseous metastasis raises concern for progression at that site, and the worsening lumbar back pain similarly suggests osseous disease activity. While I cannot confirm radiographic progression without imaging, the clinical picture is highly consistent with progression, and management will proceed accordingly.

Plan:
- Enzalutamide will be continued at the current dose of 160 mg daily until restaging imaging is completed, per standard practice to avoid a potential flare.
- Urgent restaging PSMA PET/CT ordered. Given this patient's clinical trajectory and the PSMA-avid disease documented on prior PET at the time of CRPC diagnosis, PSMA PET/CT is the most sensitive modality and will help characterize extent of osseous progression, nodal burden, and — critically — whether any visceral metastases have emerged given his high-risk disease features (Gleason 9, BRCA2 loss, TP53 mutation, PTEN loss, RB1 loss on prior genomic profiling).
- Right hip dedicated MRI ordered in parallel given the new right hip pain over a known right femoral neck lesion. I am concerned about structural integrity of the proximal right femur. Orthopedic oncology referral placed contingently if the MRI reveals cortical erosion or at-risk fracture morphology — prophylactic fixation may be required.
- Results to be reviewed at a planned follow-up visit or sooner by telephone if urgent findings are identified.
- Will plan to formally discontinue enzalutamide and transition to next-line therapy at the follow-up visit after imaging review.

Next-line therapy consideration — PARP inhibitor (olaparib):
Given this patient's germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22) with confirmed somatic second hit (biallelic loss) documented on his prior somatic NGS at diagnosis and confirmed on bone biopsy at the time of initial CRPC, he is an excellent candidate for olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg twice daily. This is supported by the PROfound trial data demonstrating significant radiographic progression-free survival benefit and overall survival benefit in BRCA1/2-mutated mCRPC. The eligibility is clear and the genomic rationale is compelling. We will plan to initiate olaparib following imaging confirmation of progression. ADT will be maintained throughout per standard of care.

I will also consider whether a repeat biopsy of a progressive lesion is warranted at the time of progression to evaluate for treatment resistance mechanisms and any phenotypic changes (e.g., neuroendocrine transdifferentiation), which can occur with sequential AR pathway inhibitor exposure, particularly in patients with RB1 loss.

Baseline hematologic considerations for olaparib: Hemoglobin currently 11.1 g/dL — mildly decreased, which is concerning given the known myelosuppressive potential of olaparib. Will check iron studies and reticulocyte count today and address any nutritional deficiencies before initiating. Will set monitoring plan with CBC every 4 weeks for the first 3 months on olaparib.

2. BONE PAIN MANAGEMENT — WORSENING LUMBAR AND NEW RIGHT HIP PAIN

- Continue scheduled acetaminophen 1000 mg TID.
- Given the pain intensity and near-fall episode, initiating low-dose oxycodone 5 mg immediate-release as needed (up to every 6 hours) for breakthrough pain, with plan to reassess at next visit.
- Bowel regimen provided with opioid prescription (docusate/senna).
- Fall risk is elevated in this patient — reminded patient and his accompanying daughter regarding fall precautions, assistive device use (cane for right hip pain), and to continue safe ambulation.
- Denosumab 120 mg subcutaneous every 4 weeks will be continued (next dose on schedule). Ensure continued calcium and vitamin D supplementation.
- Dental hygiene counseling reinforced to minimize risk of osteonecrosis of the jaw from denosumab (patient has been compliant with dental follow-up).

3. ANEMIA

- Hemoglobin 11.1 g/dL — clinically Grade 1, non-symptomatic at this time.
- Iron studies and reticulocyte count ordered.
- If iron-deficient, will administer IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) as an outpatient prior to olaparib initiation.
- Will monitor closely on olaparib given its class-effect myelosuppression risk.

4. DIABETES / HYPERTENSION / HYPERLIPIDEMIA

- HbA1c 7.1% — well-controlled on metformin; no changes needed.
- Blood pressure 138/82 mmHg — adequately controlled on amlodipine/lisinopril; no changes.
- Lipids managed with atorvastatin; last lipid panel within goal.

5. GOALS OF CARE AND EMOTIONAL SUPPORT

- Mr. Kowalski and his daughter were present for this visit. The conversation today was substantive. I reviewed the trajectory of his disease — he has progressed through ADT plus abiraterone and now enzalutamide, and we are now at a third-line therapeutic decision point. I was direct in conveying that while olaparib offers meaningful and well-supported benefit given his BRCA2 status, his disease is incurable and the goal of treatment remains life-prolongation and quality of life preservation. He asked thoughtful questions and expressed a clear desire to pursue active treatment. His daughter expressed concerns about cumulative toxicity and asked about palliative care integration — I emphasized that palliative care is not synonymous with hospice and offered a formal referral to our supportive care team, which both patient and daughter accepted. Palliative care referral placed.
- Will revisit goals of care and advance directive documentation at next visit.

RETURN: Follow-up in approximately 2-3 weeks for imaging review and initiation of next-line therapy (anticipated olaparib). Patient instructed to call the oncology triage line immediately for fever, severe pain, falls with injury, leg weakness, or urinary/bowel dysfunction.

Time spent: 55 minutes, majority in counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed by: [Attending, Genitourinary Medical Oncology]
